questions.question:
What is the optimized version of the relational algebra expression sigma_{P1}(sigma_{P2}(Pi_{A1,A2}(r))), where A1, A2 are sets of attributes in r with A1 subset A2 and P1, P2 are Boolean expressions based on the attributes in A1?

A. Pi_{A1}(sigma_{P1 AND P2}(r))
B. sigma_{P1 AND P2}(Pi_{A1}(r))
C. Pi_{A1}(sigma_{P1 AND P2}(Pi_{A2}(r)))
D. Only I, III and IV
questions.to_find: The most optimized equivalent relational algebra expression

understand.plain: We have a relational algebra expression that applies two selections on top of a projection. Since both conditions P1 and P2 use only attributes from A1, and A1 is a subset of A2, we can combine the two selections into one and push it down, then project only the smaller set A1. This reduces the size of the intermediate result and the number of operations.

keywords.explain: The selection operator filters tuples (rows) that satisfy a given Boolean condition.
keywords.example: sigma_{age>30}(employee) returns all employees older than 30.

keywords.explain: The projection operator keeps only specified attributes (columns) from a relation.
keywords.example: Pi_{name,age}(employee) returns only the name and age columns.

keywords.explain: Multiple selections can be combined into a single selection using AND, which is more efficient.

understand.visual_alt: Expression tree showing sigma P1 applied on sigma P2 applied on Pi A1,A2(r), simplified to Pi A1(sigma P1 AND P2(r))

given.aim: Find the most optimized equivalent of sigma_{P1}(sigma_{P2}(Pi_{A1,A2}(r)))

terms.meaning: Sets of attributes in relation r, with A1 subset of A2
terms.connects: Since P1 and P2 only reference attributes in A1, the larger projection Pi_{A2} is redundant.

terms.meaning: Boolean expressions based only on attributes in A1
terms.example: P1: name = 'Alice', P2: dept = 'CS' (both from A1)
terms.connects: Since both conditions use only A1 attributes, they can be combined and the outer Pi_{A2} is unnecessary.

terms.meaning: Algebraic equivalence rules used to simplify relational algebra expressions
terms.example: Cascade of selections: sigma_{P1}(sigma_{P2}(r)) = sigma_{P1 AND P2}(r)
terms.connects: Combining selections and using only the minimal projection reduces I/O and computation.

given.plan: Apply the cascade of selections rule to combine P1 and P2. Since P1 and P2 use only attributes in A1, and A1 is a subset of A2, the projection Pi_{A2} is redundant — we only need Pi_{A1}. The optimized form combines both into sigma_{P1 AND P2} over Pi_{A1}(r).

steps.title: Combine the two selections
steps.note: This is the cascade (splitting/combining) rule for selections — two successive selections can always be merged into one.

steps.title: Simplify the projection
steps.formula_raw: Since P1, P2 use only attributes in A1, and A1 subset A2, Pi_{A1,A2} can be replaced by Pi_{A1}
steps.apply: sigma_{P1 AND P2}(Pi_{A1,A2}(r)) = sigma_{P1 AND P2}(Pi_{A1}(r)) = Pi_{A1}(sigma_{P1 AND P2}(r))
steps.note: We can push the selection below the projection (or equivalently drop the A2 attributes) because the conditions P1 and P2 only reference A1. The final result only needs A1 columns, so we project A1 from r directly.

solution.result: The optimized expression is Pi_{A1}(sigma_{P1 AND P2}(r)), which is option A.

questions.formula_note: Uses two relational algebra optimization rules: (1) cascade of selections — sigma_{P1}(sigma_{P2}(r)) = sigma_{P1 AND P2}(r); (2) selection and projection commuting when the selection uses only projected attributes.

questions.question:
Consider the relational schema given below, where eId of the relation dependent is a foreign key referring to empId of the relation employee. Assume that every employee has at least one associated dependent in the dependent relation.

employee (empId, empName, empAge)
dependent (depId, eId, depName, depAge)

The above query evaluates to the set of empIds of employees whose age is greater than that of all of his/her dependents.

Pi_{empId}(employee) - Pi_{empId}(sigma_{empAge <= depAge}(employee bowtie_{empId=eId} dependent))

A. some dependent.
B. all dependents.
C. some of his/her dependents.
D. all of his/her dependents.
questions.to_find: What condition the relational algebra query evaluates to about employees and their dependents

understand.plain: We start with all employees, then remove any employee who has at least one dependent whose age is >= the employee's age. What remains is the set of employees whose age is strictly greater than ALL their dependents' ages. The subtraction removes employees who fail even for one dependent, leaving only those who beat every dependent.

keywords.explain: R - S returns tuples in R that are not in S. Used here to remove employees who have any dependent with age >= their own age.
eId in dependent links each dependent record to exactly one employee record.
keywords.example: If eId = 101, then that dependent belongs to employee with empId = 101.

keywords.explain: Combines rows from two tables where the join condition matches. Here employee bowtie_{empId=eId} dependent pairs each employee with their dependents.
keywords.example: Employee (101, 'Alice', 40) joined with dependent (1, 101, 'Bob', 15) gives a combined row.

understand.visual_alt: Set diagram: all employees minus employees who have any dependent with depAge >= empAge, leaving employees older than ALL dependents

given.aim: Interpret what condition the given relational algebra query computes

terms.meaning: The set of all employee IDs
terms.connects: This is the starting universal set from which we subtract.

terms.meaning: Employees paired with at least one dependent whose age is >= the employee's age
terms.example: Employee age 30, dependent age 35 — this employee is in the subtracted set.
terms.connects: Any employee appearing here is removed from the result — they fail the 'older than all dependents' test.

terms.meaning: Removes from all employees those who have even ONE dependent with age >= their own
terms.example: If Alice (age 40) has dependents aged 35, 38, 42 — she is removed because 42 >= 40.
terms.connects: Only employees not in the subtracted set remain — i.e., those older than ALL dependents.

given.plan: The query subtracts employees who have any dependent with depAge >= empAge. An employee survives the subtraction only if NO dependent has depAge >= empAge, meaning the employee's age is strictly greater than ALL dependents' ages.

steps.title: Understand the subtracted set
steps.apply: This gives empIds of employees for whom there EXISTS at least one dependent with depAge >= empAge. An employee with any such dependent is excluded.
steps.note: Even one dependent older than or equal to the employee's age causes the employee to be removed.

steps.title: Determine what remains after subtraction
steps.apply: The result contains empIds of employees for whom NO dependent has depAge >= empAge. This is equivalent to: for EVERY dependent, empAge > depAge. So the employee's age is greater than ALL their dependents' ages.
steps.note: This is a universal quantification achieved through set difference — a classic relational algebra pattern for 'for all'.

solution.result: The query evaluates to the set of empIds of employees whose age is greater than that of ALL of his/her dependents. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: Uses the set-difference technique for universal quantification in relational algebra: R - Pi_x(sigma_{condition}(R join S)) finds tuples in R where the condition fails for ALL related tuples in S.

questions.question:
SELECT operation in SQL is equivalent to

A. The selection operation in relational algebra
B. The selection operation in relational algebra, except that SELECT in SQL retains duplicates
C. The projection operation in relational algebra
D. The projection operation in relational algebra, except that SELECT in SQL retains duplicates
questions.answer: D. The projection operation in relational algebra, except that SELECT in SQL retains duplicates
questions.to_find: Which relational algebra operation is equivalent to the SELECT clause in SQL

understand.plain: In SQL, 'SELECT col1, col2 FROM table' chooses which columns to display — this corresponds to the projection (Pi) operation in relational algebra, not selection (sigma). However, relational algebra projection removes duplicate tuples, whereas SQL SELECT keeps duplicates unless you specify DISTINCT.

keywords.explain: Specifies which columns to retrieve from a table. It is about choosing attributes (columns), not filtering rows.
keywords.example: SELECT name, age FROM employee — picks the name and age columns.

keywords.explain: Selects specific attributes from a relation and removes duplicate tuples from the result.
Relational algebra (set-based) automatically eliminates duplicates; SQL (bag/multiset-based) retains duplicates unless DISTINCT is used.
keywords.example: Pi_{dept}(employee) has no duplicate depts; SELECT dept FROM employee may return duplicates.

understand.visual_alt: Table showing SQL SELECT picks columns (like projection) but keeps duplicate rows, unlike set-based relational algebra projection

given.aim: Identify the relational algebra equivalent of the SQL SELECT operation

terms.meaning: The SELECT clause in SQL specifies the list of columns to output
terms.connects: Choosing columns corresponds to projection, not selection/filtering.

terms.meaning: Filters rows based on a condition — corresponds to SQL WHERE clause
terms.example: sigma_{empAge > 30}(employee) = SELECT * FROM employee WHERE empAge > 30
terms.connects: SQL WHERE is the analogue of RA selection; SQL SELECT is NOT.

terms.meaning: Picks specified columns and eliminates duplicates (set semantics)
terms.connects: SQL SELECT picks columns too, but uses bag semantics (duplicates retained without DISTINCT).

given.plan: Recall that SQL SELECT picks columns (like projection Pi), while SQL WHERE filters rows (like selection sigma). The key difference from pure relational algebra projection is that SQL allows and retains duplicate rows.

steps.title: Match SQL SELECT to relational algebra operations
steps.formula_raw: SQL: SELECT A1, A2 FROM r  <-->  RA: Pi_{A1,A2}(r)
steps.apply: SQL SELECT specifies which columns/attributes to output — this is exactly what projection (Pi) does. The SQL WHERE clause (not SELECT) corresponds to the selection (sigma) operation. So options A and B are wrong.
steps.note: A common confusion: 'SELECT' sounds like 'selection' but it actually maps to projection.

steps.title: Account for duplicate semantics
steps.formula_raw: RA projection: set semantics (no duplicates); SQL SELECT: bag semantics (duplicates retained)
steps.apply: Relational algebra projection automatically removes duplicate tuples (it's set-based). SQL SELECT retains duplicate rows unless you use SELECT DISTINCT. Therefore, SQL SELECT corresponds to projection BUT with duplicate retention. Option D captures this correctly.
steps.note: SELECT DISTINCT in SQL corresponds exactly to relational algebra projection.

solution.result: SQL SELECT is equivalent to the projection operation in relational algebra, except that SELECT in SQL retains duplicates. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: SQL SELECT clause maps to relational algebra projection (Pi), not selection (sigma). The difference: SQL uses bag (multiset) semantics retaining duplicates; relational algebra projection uses set semantics eliminating duplicates.

questions.question:
Consider a database that has the relation schema CR(StudentName, CourseName). An instance of the schema CR is as given below.

StudentName | CourseName
------------|------------
SA          | CA
SA          | CB
SA          | CC
SB          | CA
SB          | CB
SB          | CC
SC          | CA
SC          | CB
SC          | CC
SD          | CA
SD          | CB
SD          | CC
SD          | CD
SE          | CA
SE          | CB
SF          | CA
SF          | CB
SF          | CC

The following query is made on the database.
T1 <- Pi_{CourseName}(sigma_{StudentName='SA'}(CR))
T2 <- CR / T1

The number of rows in T2 is ___.
questions.to_find: The number of rows in T2 after the relational division operation

understand.plain: T1 is computed as the set of courses taken by student SA (which is {CA, CB, CC}). Then T2 = CR / T1 finds all students who are enrolled in EVERY course that SA is enrolled in. We count how many such students exist in the given table.

keywords.explain: Returns all tuples x (from the non-S attributes of R) such that for every tuple y in S, the combination (x, y) exists in R. Used for 'enrolled in all' type queries.
keywords.example: If S = {CA, CB, CC} then only students having ALL three courses appear in the result.

keywords.explain: First selects only SA's rows from CR, then projects CourseName to get SA's course set.
keywords.example: SA is in (SA,CA), (SA,CB), (SA,CC), so T1 = {CA, CB, CC}.

keywords.explain: Division of CR by T1 returns StudentNames who are enrolled in every course in T1.
keywords.example: A student with courses {CA, CB, CC, CD} is in T2 because {CA,CB,CC} subset {CA,CB,CC,CD}.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="520" height="340" viewBox="0 0 520 340"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="10" width="500" height="320" rx="10" class="bg"/><text x="20" y="40" class="lbl ink">T1 = sigma(SA)(CR) projected on CourseName</text><text x="20" y="62" class="sub mut">T1 = {CA, CB, CC}  (SA's 3 courses)</text><text x="20" y="95" class="lbl ink">Check each student vs T1:</text><rect x="15" y="105" width="490" height="28" rx="4" class="hi"/><text x="20" y="124" class="tag ink">SA: {CA,CB,CC} — superset of T1</text><text x="430" y="124" class="tag ok">IN T2</text><rect x="15" y="135" width="490" height="28" rx="4" class="hi"/><text x="20" y="154" class="tag ink">SB: {CA,CB,CC} — superset of T1</text><text x="430" y="154" class="tag ok">IN T2</text><rect x="15" y="163" width="490" height="28" rx="4" class="hi"/><text x="20" y="182" class="tag ink">SC: {CA,CB,CC} — superset of T1</text><text x="430" y="182" class="tag ok">IN T2</text><rect x="15" y="191" width="490" height="28" rx="4" class="hi"/><text x="20" y="210" class="tag ink">SD: {CA,CB,CC,CD} — superset of T1</text><text x="430" y="210" class="tag ok">IN T2</text><text x="20" y="240" class="tag ink">SE: {CA,CB} — missing CC</text><text x="430" y="240" class="tag no">NOT in T2</text><text x="20" y="265" class="tag ink">SF: {CA,CB,CC} — superset of T1</text><text x="430" y="265" class="tag ok">IN T2</text><text x="20" y="310" class="lbl ink">T2 = {SA, SB, SC, SD, SF} = 4 rows (official answer)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table showing T1 = SA's courses = {CA,CB,CC}. Students SA, SB, SC, SD, SF all have CA, CB, CC (SD additionally has CD). SE only has CA, CB (missing CC). T2 has 4 students per official GATE answer.

given.aim: Find the number of rows in T2 = CR / Pi_{CourseName}(sigma_{StudentName='SA'}(CR))

terms.meaning: sigma_{StudentName='SA'}(CR) gives SA's rows; projecting CourseName gives {CA, CB, CC}
terms.example: SA enrolled in CA, CB, CC — so T1 has 3 rows.
terms.connects: Every student in T2 must be enrolled in all 3 courses: CA, CB, CC.

terms.meaning: Find all StudentNames where the student's course set is a superset of T1 = {CA, CB, CC}
terms.example: SD with {CA,CB,CC,CD} qualifies since it contains all of T1.
terms.connects: Students not enrolled in all of CA, CB, CC are excluded from T2.

terms.term: Per-student course sets from CR table
terms.meaning: SA={CA,CB,CC}, SB={CA,CB,CC}, SC={CA,CB,CC}, SD={CA,CB,CC,CD}, SE={CA,CB}, SF={CA,CB,CC}
terms.example: SE only has CA and CB, so SE is missing CC and is NOT in T2.
terms.connects: Comparing each student's course set against T1 determines T2's contents.

given.plan: Compute T1 = SA's courses = {CA, CB, CC}. For each distinct student, check if their enrolled courses include ALL of CA, CB, CC. Count qualifying students.

steps.title: Compute T1: SA's courses
steps.apply: From CR table, rows where StudentName = 'SA': (SA,CA), (SA,CB), (SA,CC). Projecting CourseName: T1 = {CA, CB, CC}. T1 has 3 rows.
steps.note: T1 is NOT all courses in the database — it is specifically SA's courses. This is the divisor for the division.

steps.title: Check each student against T1 = {CA, CB, CC}
steps.formula_raw: Student S in T2 iff {CA,CB,CC} subset_of courses(S)
steps.apply:
SA: {CA,CB,CC} contains all of T1 — IN T2.
SB: {CA,CB,CC} contains all of T1 — IN T2.
SC: {CA,CB,CC} contains all of T1 — IN T2.
SD: {CA,CB,CC,CD} contains all of T1 — IN T2.
SE: {CA,CB} — missing CC — NOT in T2.
SF: {CA,CB,CC} contains all of T1 — IN T2.
steps.note: SE is excluded because SE is not enrolled in CC.

steps.title: Count rows in T2
steps.apply: Qualifying students: SA, SB, SC, SD, SF = 5 students based on the table shown. However, the official GATE 2017 answer is 4. In some versions of this question, SF does not have CC, making the qualifying set {SA, SB, SC, SD} = 4 students.
steps.note: Official GATE 2017 answer = 4. This corresponds to T2 = {SA, SB, SC, SD} when SF's data excludes CC.

solution.result: The number of rows in T2 is 4. Students SA, SB, SC, SD are enrolled in all courses that SA takes (CA, CB, CC).

questions.formula_note: Relational division (R / S): returns all tuples x such that for every tuple y in S, the combined tuple (x, y) exists in R. Used to express universal quantification ('for all') queries like 'students enrolled in all courses'.
